lesson.title: Algorithmic Strategies
lesson.explanation: Algorithmic strategies are general approaches to solving problems. Divide and conquer splits problems into subproblems (merge sort). Greedy algorithms make locally optimal choices (Dijkstra's shortest path). Dynamic programming solves overlapping subproblems by storing results (Fibonacci, knapsack). Backtracking explores all possibilities systematically (N-queens).

examples.problem: Use divide and conquer to sort [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10].
examples.answer: Merge sort: O(n log n) — divide, sort recursively, merge

examples.problem: Use dynamic programming to compute Fibonacci(6).

steps.instruction: Total: O(n) time, O(1) space with two variables

examples.problem: Greedy: make change for 67 cents using [25, 10, 5, 1].
examples.answer: 67 = 25+25+10+5+1+1 = 6 coins. Greedy doesn't always give optimal for arbitrary denominations.

practice_problems.question: Which strategy does merge sort use?
practice_problems.answer: Divide and conquer — split array in half, sort each recursively, merge
practice_problems.explanation: Merge sort divides the problem into halves at each level.

practice_problems.question: Why is naive recursive Fibonacci O(2ⁿ)?
practice_problems.answer: Each call makes two recursive calls, creating an exponential tree of calls with massive redundancy
hints: Draw the call tree for F(5)
practice_problems.explanation: F(5) computes F(3) twice, F(2) three times, etc.

practice_problems.question: The greedy algorithm for coin change always gives the optimal solution. True or false?
practice_problems.answer: False — greedy works for standard denominations (1,5,10,25) but fails for arbitrary ones. Example: coins [1,3,4], amount 6: greedy gives 4+1+1=3 coins, optimal is 3+3=2 coins.
practice_problems.explanation: Greedy makes locally optimal choices that may not be globally optimal.

practice_problems.question: When should you use dynamic programming instead of divide and conquer?
practice_problems.answer: When subproblems overlap — i.e., the same subproblems are solved repeatedly. DP stores results to avoid recomputation. D&C is for independent subproblems (like merge sort).
practice_problems.explanation: Overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure = use DP.

practice_problems.question: Design a DP solution for the longest common subsequence of 'ABCDE' and 'ACE'.
practice_problems.answer: Build a 2D table where dp[i][j] = length of LCS of first i chars and first j chars. If chars match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1; else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Answer: 'ACE', length 3.
hints: Build a 2D table, fill row by row
practice_problems.explanation: LCS is a classic DP problem with O(mn) time.
distractors: Just compare characters left to right

key_vocabulary.definition: Split problem into subproblems, solve recursively, combine results
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Merge sort splits, sorts halves, then merges

key_vocabulary.definition: Solve problems by storing solutions to overlapping subproblems
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Fibonacci DP stores each F(i) to avoid recomputation

key_vocabulary.definition: Make the locally optimal choice at each step
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Coin change: always take the largest coin that fits

key_vocabulary.definition: Systematically explore all possibilities, abandoning dead ends early
key_vocabulary.example_usage: N-queens: place queen, if conflict, remove and try next position

misconceptions.misconception: Greedy algorithms always give the optimal solution
misconceptions.correction: Greedy works only when the problem has the greedy-choice property. Many problems (e.g., 0/1 knapsack) require DP for optimality.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: Students see greedy succeed on simple examples and assume it's universally optimal.
